title: Build a Mini-Unit Map
useCase: Plan a small unit before writing individual lessons.
prompt: Act as a curriculum planning partner. I am building a mini-unit map for [subject and grade band]. Topic: [topic]. Time available: [number of class periods]. Standards or skills: [paste exact standards or teacher-written skills]. Required assessment or product: [assessment]. Create a concise mini-unit map with an essential question, 3 to 5 learning targets, lesson sequence, formative checks, required resources, and one revision note. Do not invent standards or sources. Mark anything that needs teacher verification.
expectedOutput: A compact mini-unit map with learning targets, sequence, checks for understanding, resources, and a revision note.
verificationChecklist: Learning targets match the supplied standards or skills.
The pacing is realistic for the class periods available.
No unsupported standards or sources were invented.
The assessment matches the learning targets.
The plan is usable in a real classroom.
revisionStep: Revise the map so the first lesson, assessment, and final artifact all point to the same learning targets.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not include student-identifiable information, private school data, private links, or copyrighted text you are not allowed to reuse.

title: Convert Standards Into Learning Targets
useCase: Turn exact standard text into teacher-usable targets.
prompt: Act as a standards unpacking assistant. Use only the standard text I provide. Standard text: [paste exact standard]. Subject and grade band: [subject and grade]. Course context: [course or unit]. Restate the standard in plain language, identify the key verbs and nouns, write 3 to 5 measurable learning targets, suggest one evidence check for each target, and flag vocabulary students may need. If the standard text is unclear, say what needs manual review instead of guessing.
expectedOutput: A standards unpacking note with plain-language meaning, verbs, nouns, learning targets, evidence checks, and vocabulary.
verificationChecklist: Every target traces to the supplied standard.
No new standard code or requirement was invented.
Targets use observable verbs.
Evidence checks are appropriate for the grade band.
Teacher review items are clearly marked.
revisionStep: Replace any vague target with an observable student action and a matching evidence check.
safetyPrivacyNote: Use public standard text or teacher notes only. Do not paste private student performance data.

title: Create a Lesson Outline From a Teacher Goal
useCase: Draft a teachable lesson outline from a clear goal.
prompt: Act as a practical lesson planning assistant. Teacher goal: [goal]. Grade band and subject: [grade and subject]. Class length: [minutes]. Students already know: [prior knowledge]. Materials available: [materials]. Draft a lesson outline with objective, hook, short explanation, guided practice, independent task, check for understanding, and closure. Keep directions concrete. Do not add facts, standards, or resources unless I provide them.
expectedOutput: A lesson outline with classroom flow, timing suggestions, student task, and evidence of learning.
verificationChecklist: The lesson goal is visible in every major activity.
Timing fits the class length.
The student task produces observable evidence.
No facts or resources were invented.
Directions are clear enough to teach from.
revisionStep: Cut or combine steps until the lesson can realistically run in the available time.
safetyPrivacyNote: Use generic classroom context. Do not include private student needs, names, grades, or behavior records.

title: Generate Student-Facing Directions
useCase: Turn a teacher task into clear student directions.
prompt: Act as a classroom writing assistant. Rewrite this task as student-facing directions. Task: [describe task]. Grade band: [grade]. Submission method: [how students submit]. Success criteria: [criteria]. Reading level target: [level]. Write a short overview, numbered steps, what to submit, success criteria, and a help section. Use plain language and second person. Do not add requirements that I did not provide.
expectedOutput: Student directions with overview, steps, submission instructions, success criteria, and help notes.
verificationChecklist: Students can tell what to do first.
Submission requirements are explicit.
Success criteria match the teacher task.
Language is appropriate for the reading level.
No extra requirements were invented.
revisionStep: Read the directions as a student and rewrite any step that contains more than one action.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not include private student examples, names, grades, or links that require restricted access.

title: Draft an Analytic Rubric
useCase: Create a first rubric draft from targets and an assignment.
prompt: Act as an assessment design assistant. Assignment: [assignment]. Learning targets: [targets]. Grade band: [grade]. Point scale: [scale]. Draft an analytic rubric with 3 to 5 criteria, 4 performance levels, observable descriptors, and a short student-friendly note. Criteria must come from the supplied targets. Avoid vague words such as good, excellent, weak, or effort unless they are defined by observable evidence.
expectedOutput: An analytic rubric table with criteria, performance levels, descriptors, and student-facing note.
verificationChecklist: Each criterion maps to a learning target.
Descriptors describe observable student work.
Performance levels are meaningfully different.
The rubric does not reward compliance instead of learning.
Point values are clear.
revisionStep: Replace vague descriptors with visible evidence a teacher can actually score.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not paste private student work, names, scores, or identifying details. Use a generic assignment description or de-identified sample.

title: Check a Rubric for Observable Criteria
useCase: Review a rubric before students see it.
prompt: Act as a rubric reviewer. Rubric draft: [paste rubric]. Assignment purpose: [purpose]. Learning targets: [targets]. Check whether each criterion and descriptor is observable, aligned, student-friendly, and scoreable. Create a findings table with issue, why it matters, suggested revision, and priority. Do not rewrite the whole rubric unless a revision is clearly needed.
expectedOutput: A rubric review table plus targeted revisions for unclear criteria or descriptors.
verificationChecklist: The review references the supplied rubric.
Each suggested change improves observability or alignment.
No new targets are added.
Student-facing language remains clear.
Scoring expectations are less ambiguous after revision.
revisionStep: Apply only the revisions that make scoring clearer, then reread the rubric from a student perspective.
safetyPrivacyNote: Remove student names, scores, and private feedback before using this prompt.

title: Verify AI-Generated Facts
useCase: Audit a draft for claims that need source checks.
prompt: Act as a fact-check planning assistant. AI draft: [paste draft]. Subject area: [subject]. Trusted sources or required source type: [sources]. Identify factual claims, dates, statistics, definitions, and citations that need verification. Make a table with claim, why it matters, source needed, status, and suggested teacher action. Do not claim a fact is verified unless I provided the source evidence.
expectedOutput: A verification table that separates supported claims, unsupported claims, and manual checks.
verificationChecklist: Every important factual claim is listed.
Unsupported claims are marked for teacher review.
The response does not invent citations.
Source needs are specific.
Student-facing use is blocked until review is complete.
revisionStep: Remove or rewrite any claim that cannot be verified from an acceptable source.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not paste private student work, internal documents, or copyrighted excerpts beyond what policy permits.

title: Check Output for Teacher Voice
useCase: Revise generic AI text into usable teacher language.
prompt: Act as a teacher-voice revision partner. Draft text: [paste draft]. Audience: [students, families, colleagues, or self]. My tone should be: [tone]. Classroom context: [context]. Identify generic, robotic, inaccurate, or too-formal lines. Then revise the text so it sounds clear, practical, and appropriate for the audience. Preserve facts I supplied. Mark any factual claim that still needs verification.
expectedOutput: A short revision report and a cleaner version in the teacher's stated voice.
verificationChecklist: The revised text keeps the teacher's intent.
Generic AI filler is removed.
Factual claims needing review are marked.
The audience is appropriate.
The teacher can edit the final version quickly.
revisionStep: Read the revised version aloud and change any sentence you would not actually say or publish.
safetyPrivacyNote: Remove private student, family, staff, or school details before sharing the draft with an AI tool.

title: Create a Source Evaluation Note
useCase: Evaluate whether a source belongs in a lesson or unit.
prompt: Act as a source evaluation assistant. Source title: [title]. Source link or citation: [link or citation]. Lesson purpose: [purpose]. Known license or terms: [license or unknown]. Create a source evaluation note covering relevance, credibility, reading level, bias or limitations, license or reuse concern, and use decision. If the license or credibility cannot be confirmed from what I provide, mark it as needs manual check.
expectedOutput: A source note with relevance, credibility, license status, limitations, and use decision.
verificationChecklist: The source is relevant to the lesson purpose.
Credibility claims are not overstated.
License status is clear or marked for review.
Limitations are named.
The use decision is practical.
revisionStep: Add the final source note to the resource evaluation sheet only after checking the link and license yourself.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not paste paywalled text, copyrighted passages, private documents, or student data into the prompt.

title: Build a Google Drive Folder Plan
useCase: Plan a maintainable folder structure for a course or unit.
prompt: Act as a Google Workspace planning assistant. Course or unit: [name]. Ownership model: [individual, team, department, or school-managed]. File types: [Docs, Slides, Forms, Sheets, PDFs, images]. Archive needs: [semester, unit, yearly]. Propose a simple Drive folder plan with folder names, purpose, owner type, sharing notes, archive rule, and naming convention. Keep it small enough for a teacher to maintain.
expectedOutput: A folder plan with folder names, ownership notes, sharing rules, archive rule, and naming convention.
verificationChecklist: Folder names are understandable.
Ownership is explicit.
Sharing notes avoid overexposure.
Archive rules are realistic.
The plan does not require unnecessary folders.
revisionStep: Remove any folder that does not have a clear maintenance owner or classroom purpose.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not paste private Drive links, student names, rosters, grades, or account details.

title: Create a Google Sheets Curriculum Tracker Plan
useCase: Design the columns and workflow for a curriculum tracker.
prompt: Act as a teacher workflow designer. Course: [course]. Tracker purpose: [purpose]. Decisions this Sheet must support: [decisions]. Users: [teacher, team, department]. Design a Google Sheets curriculum tracker with tabs, column names, required values, update rhythm, and review questions. Keep the tracker focused on decisions, not data collection for its own sake.
expectedOutput: A Sheets tracker design with tabs, columns, update rhythm, and review questions.
verificationChecklist: Every column supports a teacher decision.
The Sheet avoids private student-level data unless absolutely required.
Update rhythm is realistic.
Users and owner are clear.
Review questions are actionable.
revisionStep: Cut columns that would be filled once and never used for a decision.
safetyPrivacyNote: Avoid student-identifiable data. Use aggregate or planning-level information whenever possible.

title: Plan a Google Form Check for Understanding
useCase: Design a short Form that gives useful formative evidence.
teacherContextNeeded: What teacher will do with results
prompt: Act as a formative assessment planner. Learning target: [target]. Question count: [number]. Response types allowed: [multiple choice, short answer, scale, checkbox]. How I will use results: [teacher action]. Draft a Google Form plan with student directions, questions, answer expectations, response review plan, and follow-up action. Keep the Form short and aligned to the target.
expectedOutput: A Google Form plan with directions, aligned questions, response review notes, and follow-up action.
verificationChecklist: Each question checks the learning target.
The Form is short enough for the class routine.
Response review has a clear teacher action.
Questions are student-friendly.
Sensitive data is not requested.
revisionStep: Remove questions that are interesting but do not change the teacher's next instructional move.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not ask students to submit private personal information, passwords, family details, or sensitive disclosures.

title: Draft an Accessibility Review
useCase: Review a lesson, page, slide deck, or handout for accessibility concerns.
prompt: Act as an accessibility review partner. Material summary: [summary]. Audience: [grade band and learners]. Format: [Doc, slide deck, website, worksheet, video, or other]. Known constraints: [constraints]. Review for readable structure, headings, contrast, alt text needs, captions or transcript needs, link clarity, reading load, and flexible access. Return a table of issues, why they matter, suggested fix, and priority.
expectedOutput: An accessibility review table with prioritized fixes.
verificationChecklist: The review matches the material format.
Fixes are practical for a teacher.
Accessibility needs are not treated as decoration.
Student privacy is preserved.
High-priority blockers are clear.
revisionStep: Apply high-priority fixes first, then test whether a student can follow the material without teacher explanation.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not paste private student accommodation records, IEP details, medical information, or learner profiles.

title: Rewrite Directions for Clarity
useCase: Make directions shorter, clearer, and easier to follow.
prompt: Act as a clarity editor for classroom directions. Current directions: [paste directions]. Grade band: [grade]. Task goal: [goal]. Must-keep requirements: [requirements]. Rewrite the directions with short sentences, numbered steps, clear verbs, and a what-to-submit section. Then list what you changed and what the teacher should verify.
expectedOutput: Rewritten directions plus a change note and teacher verification items.
verificationChecklist: Each step starts with a clear action.
The rewrite keeps required information.
No deadlines or requirements were invented.
Language fits the grade band.
What to submit is unambiguous.
revisionStep: Ask a colleague to identify the first action and final submission from the rewritten directions.
safetyPrivacyNote: Remove private student examples, grades, or restricted links before using this prompt.

title: Create a Substitute-Friendly Lesson Packet
useCase: Prepare a lesson another adult can run safely.
prompt: Act as a substitute lesson packet assistant. Lesson goal: [goal]. Class length: [minutes]. Materials: [materials]. Student task: [task]. Classroom routines that are safe to share: [routines]. Create a substitute-friendly packet with lesson overview, timing, student directions, materials checklist, what to collect, common student questions, and teacher follow-up note. Do not include private behavior plans or student-specific information.
expectedOutput: A substitute packet with timing, directions, collection notes, and follow-up guidance.
verificationChecklist: The packet can be followed without teacher memory.
Private student information is excluded.
Materials and collection steps are clear.
Timing is realistic.
The follow-up note tells the teacher what to review.
revisionStep: Remove any instruction that depends on knowing individual students or private classroom context.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not include private student-specific behavior, medical, disability, grade, or family information.

title: Plan a Course Site Homepage
useCase: Plan the first page of a teacher course site.
prompt: Act as a course site planning assistant. Course: [course]. Audience: [students, families, teachers, public]. Required links: [links or sections]. Update rhythm: [daily, weekly, unit]. Public/private boundary: [what must stay private]. Plan a course site homepage with page sections, navigation labels, student-first copy, maintenance notes, and safety checks. Keep it small and maintainable.
expectedOutput: A course site homepage plan with sections, navigation, copy guidance, maintenance notes, and safety checks.
verificationChecklist: Homepage sections support the audience.
Private materials are not placed in public sections.
Navigation labels are clear.
Maintenance rhythm is realistic.
Accessibility and source notes are included.
revisionStep: Remove any section that will not be maintained during a normal teaching week.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not expose private class links, student work, schedules, rosters, or internal systems on a public site.

title: Create a Privacy and Safety Review Checklist
useCase: Review a teacher-facing resource before publishing or sharing.
prompt: Act as a defensive privacy and safety reviewer for educators. Resource type: [site, Doc, slide deck, repo, Form, video, or other]. Audience: [audience]. Sharing location: [public, school-only, class-only]. Data or media involved: [summary only]. Create a checklist for student privacy, teacher privacy, school information, copyright, source accuracy, accessibility, and account safety. Mark urgent items that must be fixed before sharing.
expectedOutput: A privacy and safety checklist with urgent blockers and follow-up review items.
verificationChecklist: The checklist matches the sharing context.
Student and teacher privacy are both considered.
Copyright and source accuracy are included.
Accessibility is included.
Urgent blockers are clear.
revisionStep: Turn urgent blockers into concrete fixes before the resource is published or sent.
safetyPrivacyNote: Describe sensitive student material in general terms. Do not paste private data, secret links, tokens, or screenshots with identifiable information.

title: Draft an Apps Script Automation Requirement
useCase: Define what an automation should do before code is written.
prompt: Act as an automation requirements assistant for a teacher. Manual workflow: [workflow]. Workspace tool: [Sheets, Docs, Forms, Drive, Gmail, or Calendar]. Trigger: [manual, form submit, time-based, edit]. Inputs: [inputs]. Expected output: [output]. Failure risk: [what could go wrong]. Write a plain-language automation requirement with purpose, scope, inputs, outputs, permissions, test plan, rollback plan, and what should not be automated.
expectedOutput: A teacher-readable automation requirement with scope, permissions, test plan, and rollback plan.
verificationChecklist: The requirement describes a real repeated task.
Inputs and outputs are clear.
Permissions are named.
Test and rollback plans exist.
Unsafe or unnecessary automation is excluded.
revisionStep: Before asking for code, rewrite the requirement until a colleague can explain what the automation will and will not do.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not paste real student records, grades, emails, API keys, OAuth secrets, or private sheet links.

title: Review a Prompt Before Using Student Data
useCase: Decide whether a prompt is safe before using classroom context.
prompt: Act as a privacy reviewer for teacher AI use. Prompt draft: [paste prompt draft]. AI tool: [tool]. Purpose: [purpose]. Classroom information involved: [general description only]. Review whether the prompt is safe to use. Identify any request for student-identifiable information, private school data, credentials, copyrighted material, or sensitive context. Rewrite the prompt to use de-identified summaries or synthetic examples instead.
expectedOutput: A prompt safety review, risk rating, redaction guidance, and safer rewritten prompt.
verificationChecklist: The review flags student-identifiable data risks.
Private school data and credentials are addressed.
The safer prompt avoids sensitive details.
Policy review is recommended when needed.
The teacher can use the revised prompt without exposing private information.
revisionStep: Use the safer rewritten prompt, then verify the AI output before sharing it with students.
safetyPrivacyNote: Never paste student-identifiable information, private school data, passwords, API keys, tokens, or private links into an AI tool.

title: Create a Capstone Artifact Index
useCase: Organize final pathway evidence into a reviewable index.
prompt: Act as a capstone portfolio organizer. Capstone goal: [goal]. Artifacts completed: [list artifacts]. Audience: [reviewer, colleague, public, or self]. Publishing plan: [private, school-only, public]. Create an artifact index with artifact title, related OTS course, purpose, evidence of completion, source or safety note, revision status, and next action. Mark any item that should not be public.
expectedOutput: A capstone artifact index table with course links, evidence notes, safety notes, revision status, and next actions.
verificationChecklist: Every artifact has a purpose and course connection.
Public/private status is visible.
Evidence of completion is inspectable.
Source and safety notes are included.
Next actions are specific.
revisionStep: Move any incomplete or unsafe artifact into a private review section until it is ready.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not publish student work, private links, credentials, internal school information, or copyrighted materials without permission.

title: Plan an Open Resource README
useCase: Document a shared curriculum resource so another teacher can reuse it.
prompt: Act as a curriculum documentation assistant. Resource title: [title]. Audience: [teachers, students, public]. Included materials: [materials]. License or reuse limits: [license or unknown]. Setup steps: [steps]. Draft a README outline with purpose, contents, how to use, source and license notes, accessibility notes, privacy warnings, and maintenance contact or owner type. Mark unknown license items for manual review.
expectedOutput: A README outline that explains purpose, contents, use, source notes, license notes, accessibility, privacy, and maintenance.
verificationChecklist: The README explains who the resource is for.
Use steps are clear.
License status is not guessed.
Privacy and accessibility notes are present.
Maintenance owner type is visible.
revisionStep: Fill in missing license and source details before publishing the resource.
safetyPrivacyNote: Do not include private student data, private school links, or third-party content without a clear reuse basis.

title: Plan a GitHub Curriculum Repo Review
useCase: Prepare a teacher-owned repo for sharing or review.
prompt: Act as a defensive curriculum repo reviewer. Repo purpose: [purpose]. Files included: [file summary]. Audience: [audience]. Known risks: [risks]. Create a pre-share review checklist for README clarity, license, source notes, private data, secret files, screenshots, media metadata, accessibility, and maintenance. Do not provide exploitation steps. Focus on accidental exposure and documentation quality.
expectedOutput: A repo review checklist with documentation, safety, licensing, and maintenance checks.
verificationChecklist: The checklist covers secrets and private data.
License and source notes are included.
Screenshots and media metadata are considered.
README quality is addressed.
Review remains defensive and teacher-facing.
revisionStep: Fix urgent exposure items before sharing the repo link with students, families, or the public.
safetyPrivacyNote: Never paste tokens, .env values, private repo links, student records, or internal school URLs into an AI tool.

title: Design a Reflection and Revision Log
useCase: Capture what changed after teaching or peer review.
prompt: Act as a reflection partner for a teacher-owned artifact. Artifact: [artifact]. What happened during use: [summary]. Feedback received: [summary]. Next teaching cycle: [date or context]. Create a revision log entry with what worked, what failed, evidence observed, change to make, reason for the change, and how to verify it next time. Keep it honest and specific.
expectedOutput: A revision log entry with evidence, decision, change, rationale, and next verification step.
verificationChecklist: The reflection names concrete evidence.
The revision is specific.
The reason for change is clear.
The next verification step is doable.
The entry avoids private student details.
revisionStep: Rewrite broad reflections into one concrete change that can be checked in the next cycle.
safetyPrivacyNote: Summarize classroom evidence without student names, grades, behavior records, or private circumstances.
